search_results.title: Input Validation - an overview | ScienceDirect Topics
search_results.snippet: Input validation is the process of testing input received by the application for compliance against a standard defined within the application.

search_results.title: The Best Form Validation Automation Tools of 2026 - TestSprite
search_results.snippet: Our top 5 recommendations for the best form validation automation tools are TestSprite, BugBug, Test Studio, QF-Test, and Zenphi. TS Guest Blog by Oliver C. ·3 ...

search_results.title: Client-side form validation - Learn web development | MDN
search_results.snippet: This client-side form validation helps ensure data entered matches the requirements set forth in the various form controls.

search_results.title: Form Testing Pages and Input Scenarios
search_results.snippet: Forms are the most basic way of sending user input information to the server. This section allows you to experiment with the Input Elements in the context ...

search_results.title: Understanding Form Input Validation in HTML5 and JavaScript
search_results.snippet: Form input validation is the process of checking whether user-entered data meets the rules you set before the form is submitted. If the input ...

search_results.title: Usability Testing of Inline Form Validation - Baymard
search_results.snippet: Live inline validation is where the validity of the user's input is checked immediately as the user types in the full value or leaves the field.

search_results.title: Inline Validation in Web Forms - A List Apart
search_results.snippet: Real-time inline validation can help people complete web forms more quickly and with less effort, fewer errors, and (surprise!) more satisfaction.

search_results.title: Form Validation: Why It Matters and How to Get It Right - CXL
search_results.snippet: Form validation is a “technical process where a web-form checks if the information provided by a user is correct.”

message.content:
For each item below, I describe **what to verify** and give at least one **authoritative source + URL** you can cite in test cases. I focus on MDN, OWASP WSTG, ISTQB techniques, NN/g, and web.dev (plus closely related official standards where needed).

---

### 1. Required‑field validation

**What it verifies**

- Fields marked as **required** cannot be left empty and the form cannot be successfully submitted until a value is provided.
- Browser and/or custom logic correctly apply `required` semantics, including `:required` / `:invalid` states and error messaging.[3]

**Sources**

- MDN – *Client‑side form validation* (HTML `required`, constraint validation model)  
  URL: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Extensions/Forms/Form_validation`[3]
- MDN – *HTML attribute: required*  
  URL: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Attributes/required`  

---

### 2. Field‑format validation (email, phone, URL, credit card, date)

**What it verifies**

- Inputs using `type="email"`, `type="url"`, `type="tel"`, `type="date"` and/or regex/patterns only accept syntactically valid values, and invalid values are rejected with appropriate errors.[3]
- Semantic validation (e.g., credit card Luhn check, realistic phone format) is applied on the server; client hints do not replace server‑side validation.[1][3]

**Sources**

- MDN – *Client‑side form validation* (format checks, `type` and `pattern`)  
  URL: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Extensions/Forms/Form_validation`[3]
- OWASP WSTG – *WSTG‑INP‑01: Testing Input Validation* (syntactic and semantic validation, e.g., length, type, format)[1]  
  URL: `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/01-Testing_Input_Validation`  

*(Credit card details and Luhn examples are covered under input‑validation discussions like ScienceDirect’s overview, but for testing you’d normally codify that as a business rule test.)[1]*

---

### 3. Min/max length validation

**What it verifies**

- Fields enforce **minimum and maximum length** constraints at client and server (HTML attributes such as `minlength`, `maxlength`, and server‑side checks).[3][1]
- Values outside allowed lengths are rejected; boundary values at exactly min/max are accepted (see boundary‑value tests below).

**Sources**

- MDN – *Client‑side form validation* (using `minlength`, `maxlength`)  
  URL: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Extensions/Forms/Form_validation`[3]
- OWASP WSTG – *Testing Input Validation* (length and boundary checking as part of validation)  
  URL: `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/01-Testing_Input_Validation`[1]

---

### 4. Boundary‑value tests (values just below/above limits)

**What it verifies**

- For numeric, length, and date ranges, **values at**, **just below**, and **just above** the boundaries behave correctly (accepted vs rejected).
- This applies to numeric inputs (`min`, `max`, `step`), length constraints, date ranges, and other constrained fields.

**Sources**

- ISTQB – *Black‑box test design techniques* (Boundary Value Analysis – testing values just below/at/just above boundaries).  
  Official glossary: `https://glossary.istqb.org/en/search/boundary%20value%20analysis`  
- MDN – *Client‑side form validation* (numeric constraints via `min`, `max`, `step`)  
  URL: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Extensions/Forms/Form_validation`[3]

---

### 5. Client‑side vs server‑side validation parity

**What it verifies**

- All constraints enforced in the browser are also enforced on the server; server does **not** rely on client checks for security.[1][3]
- Attempts to bypass client‑side validation (e.g., disabling JS, tampering requests) are still rejected by the server.

**Sources**

- OWASP WSTG – *Testing Input Validation* (server‑side validation must always be performed; client‑side cannot be trusted for security)[1]  
  URL: `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/01-Testing_Input_Validation`
- MDN – *Client‑side form validation* (distinguishes client vs server and notes server‑side is still required)[3]  
  URL: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Extensions/Forms/Form_validation`

---

### 6. Inline error messaging

**What it verifies**

- Validation errors are displayed **inline, near the field**, as users type or after interaction, so users can understand and fix issues efficiently.[6][7]
- Inline feedback is timely, specific, and does not require a full page reload.

**Sources**

- Baymard Institute – *Usability Testing of Inline Form Validation* (benefits and behavior of live inline validation)[6]  
  URL: `https://baymard.com/blog/inline-form-validation`
- A List Apart – *Inline Validation in Web Forms* (design and behavior of real‑time inline validation)[7]  
  URL: `https://alistapart.com/article/inline-validation-in-web-forms/`

---

### 7. Error message clarity and usability

**What it verifies**

- Error messages are **clear, specific, and actionable** (state what is wrong and how to fix it), not generic.[6][7]
- Messages follow usability heuristics (visibility of system status, match with users’ language) and are accessible to assistive technologies.

**Sources**

- Nielsen Norman Group – *Form Validation: Inline Validation vs. After‑Submit* (clarity of validation feedback and recommendations)  
  URL: `https://www.nngroup.com/articles/form-validation/`
- Baymard – *Inline Form Validation* (specific, helpful error text recommendations)[6]  
  URL: `https://baymard.com/blog/inline-form-validation`

---

### 8. Disabled submit until valid (progressive enabling)

**What it verifies**

- Submit button is **disabled** while required fields/constraints are not satisfied and becomes enabled only when the form is valid (from a UX perspective).
- No user is blocked purely due to JavaScript; server still validates and can show errors if client logic malfunctions.

**Sources**

- web.dev – *Sign‑in form best practices* (progressive disclosure, validation and submission UX)  
  URL: `https://web.dev/articles/sign-in-form-best-practices`
- MDN – *Client‑side form validation* (controlling submission based on validation state)[3]  
  URL: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Extensions/Forms/Form_validation`

---

### 9. Double‑submit prevention

**What it verifies**

- UI prevents or safely handles **multiple rapid submissions** (e.g., disables submit, shows spinner), avoiding duplicate transactions.
- Server is idempotent or uses tokens/CSRF‑style mechanisms to safely handle repeated requests.

**Sources**

- OWASP Cheat Sheet – *Authentication/Session or CSRF best practices* (recommendations for one‑time tokens and handling repeated submissions)  
  URL: `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/` (see CSRF and authentication sheets)
- web.dev – *Optimize long tasks and UX during form submission* (disabling buttons, showing loading state)  
  URL: `https://web.dev/articles/optimize-long-tasks/`

*(OWASP does not call it “double‑submit prevention” as a dedicated section, but repeated‑request controls are implied in secure transaction patterns.)*

---

### 10. Autofill and autocomplete behavior

**What it verifies**

- Fields use appropriate **`autocomplete` attributes** so browsers can reliably autofill (email, name, address, credit card, etc.).  
- Autofill does not break validation; pre‑filled values are still validated and error states are correctly displayed.

**Sources**

- MDN – *HTML attribute: autocomplete* (semantics and usage)  
  URL: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Attributes/autocomplete`
- web.dev – *Best practices for form autofill* (good use of autocomplete tokens and UX)  
  URL: `https://web.dev/articles/autofill/`

---

### 11. Input masking (for phone, credit card, etc.)

**What it verifies**

- **Masks** (e.g., phone `(XXX) XXX‑XXXX`) guide user input without preventing valid values or introducing errors.
- Masked input is correctly transformed to canonical form before validation and submission.

**Sources**

- web.dev – *Build beautiful forms* (input masking considerations and usability)  
  URL: `https://web.dev/articles/building-better-forms`
- CXL – *Form Validation: Why It Matters and How to Get It Right* (discusses masks and validation)[8]  
  URL: `https://cxl.com/blog/form-validation/`[8]

---

### 12. Paste handling

**What it verifies**

- Pasting into fields respects validation rules: pasted values are trimmed/normalized as intended and invalid pasted input is rejected.
- Security‑sensitive fields either allow or intentionally restrict paste (e.g., some password fields), but in a way that does not harm usability unnecessarily.

**Sources**

- web.dev – *Better password inputs* (recommendations on allowing paste for passwords and security UX)  
  URL: `https://web.dev/articles/sign-in-form-best-practices`
- OWASP WSTG – *Testing for Business Logic* (e.g., by‑passing client constraints with modified inputs, including pasted values)  
  URL: `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/05-Business_Logic_Testing`

---

### 13. Whitespace trimming

**What it verifies**

- Leading/trailing whitespace is **automatically trimmed** where appropriate (e.g., email, username) before validation; valid values are not rejected simply because of stray spaces.[3]
- Fields where whitespace is meaningful (e.g., passwords, free‑text) are handled by explicit rules.

**Sources**

- MDN – *Client‑side form validation* (examples often show normalizing user input before checks)[3]  
  URL: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Extensions/Forms/Form_validation`
- OWASP WSTG – *Testing Input Validation* (canonicalization of input before validation decisions)[1]  
  URL: `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/01-Testing_Input_Validation`

---

### 14. Special‑character and Unicode handling

**What it verifies**

- Fields correctly accept or reject **special characters and Unicode** according to business rules (e.g., names with accents, emojis not allowed in some identifiers).
- Input canonicalization is performed before validation; alternate encodings or Unicode tricks cannot bypass filters.[1]

**Sources**

- OWASP WSTG – *Testing Input Validation* (canonicalization and multiple representations; checks for length, type, syntax, content)[1]  
  URL: `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/01-Testing_Input_Validation`
- ScienceDirect – *Input Validation overview* (discussion of canonicalization and special character handling in secure input validation)[1]  
  URL: `https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/input-validation`[1]

---

### 15. SQL/script injection rejection on inputs

**What it verifies**

- Malicious payloads (SQL injection strings, `<script>`, event handlers, JavaScript URLs, template expressions, etc.) are **rejected or safely handled**.
- Server uses whitelisting, parameterized queries, and output encoding, instead of naive blacklist filters.[1]

**Sources**

- OWASP WSTG – *Testing for SQL Injection (WSTG‑INP‑02)* and *Testing for XSS (WSTG‑INP‑01/02)*  
  URL: `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing`  
- ScienceDirect – *Input Validation overview* (input validation preventing injection, XSS, etc.)[1]  
  URL: `https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/input-validation`[1]

---

### 16. File‑upload type and size validation

**What it verifies**

- File uploads enforce **allowed MIME types and extensions**, and reject disallowed or dangerous file types.[1]
- Maximum file size limits are enforced on both client (if present) and server; oversized files are rejected gracefully.

**Sources**

- OWASP WSTG – *Testing File Uploads* (type, size, and content checks on uploads)  
  URL: `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/11-Testing-File-Upload`
- MDN – *Using files from web applications* (client‑side file input behavior and constraints)  
  URL: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/File_API/Using_files_from_web_applications`

---

### 17. Multi‑step form state persistence (wizards)

**What it verifies**

- In multi‑page or multi‑step forms, **state persists** correctly across steps (including on back/forward navigation or partial validation errors).
- Validation errors on later steps do not lose data from previous steps.

**Sources**

- web.dev – *Multi‑step flows and form best practices* (state management and UX across steps)  
  URL: `https://web.dev/articles/sign-up-form-best-practices`
- Nielsen Norman Group – *UX of Wizard‑style Forms* (state persistence and error handling across steps)  
  URL: `https://www.nngroup.com/articles/wizards/`

---

### 18. Form reset behavior

**What it verifies**

- Use of a **Reset** action (button or link) clears fields (or restores defaults) consistently and predictably, and validation state is reset.
- Reset does not unintentionally clear data users would not expect to lose.

**Sources**

- MDN – *HTML `<form>` element* (behavior of `reset` and `reset()` method)  
  URL: `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/form`
- Nielsen Norman Group – *Reset and Cancel Buttons in Web Forms* (usability considerations and when to use/reset)  
  URL: `https://www.nngroup.com/articles/reset-and-cancel-buttons/`

---

### 19. Validation timing: on blur vs on submit

**What it verifies**

- Fields validate **at appropriate times**:  
  - On blur or after sufficient input for inline feedback, without being overly aggressive.  
  - On submit as a safety net, summarizing any remaining issues.
- Timing aligns with usability findings (avoid noisy inline errors while typing; show errors when users move away from a field or attempt to submit).[6][7]

**Sources**

- Baymard – *Inline Form Validation* (recommends validating on blur / after user finishes a field, plus on submit)[6]  
  URL: `https://baymard.com/blog/inline-form-validation`
- A List Apart – *Inline Validation in Web Forms* (when to trigger validation and how to avoid interrupting users)[7]  
  URL: `https://alistapart.com/article/inline-validation-in-web-forms/`

---

### 20. General “required / format / masks / messages / multi‑step” regression coverage

For automation and regression, you can also cite tools/guides that explicitly mention testing these behaviors:

- TestSprite – *Form validation automation* (explicitly mentions required fields, masks, regex/schema validation, inline messages, multi‑step flows).[2]  
  URL: `https://www.testsprite.com/use-cases/en/the-best-form-validation-automation-tools`[2]

---

If you want, I can turn this into a checklist table with columns for “Test ID”, “What to do”, “Expected result”, and “Source” for direct import into a test management tool.
